<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: verification-bandwidth-and-context-adequacy
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/verification-bandwidth-and-context-adequacy.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/verification-bandwidth-and-context-adequacy.qmd@6013d4743
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Verification Bandwidth and Context Adequacy

Long context can make a system sound informed before it is actually able to
check what it is saying.

That is the problem this chapter names. A model may hold a large packet of
documents, memory pages, summaries, proofs, logs, and user instructions. It
may cite them, summarize them, and weave them into a fluent answer. None of
that proves it jointly compared the parts that matter for the claim at hand.
The system may have generation capacity without verification bandwidth.

Verification bandwidth is the amount of effective checking capacity available
for a specific claim. It depends on the target claim, the semantic units that
must be compared, the compression path used to fit them, the tools and proof
checks available, the review time available, and the risk level of the
decision. Context adequacy is the record that says whether the available
context is enough for that work.

The first capacity model is deliberately small, but it gives the idea a harder
edge. If twelve semantic units all have to be compared against one another,
the record has 66 comparison obligations, not twelve. A fixture that checks
18 of them leaves 48 residual obligations and must split, escalate, or stay
below verified support. A decomposition can reduce the work only when it names
the clusters and the boundary checks it still owes. Otherwise decomposition is
just a prettier way to hide the missing comparisons.

The practical lesson is simple: having the text is not the same as being able
to verify with it. A governed system should know when context is adequate for
drafting, adequate for a local check, adequate for joint verification, or
inadequate for the claim being made. When it is inadequate, the honest output
is not a stronger sentence. It is a downgrade, split, residual, retrieval
request, proof request, tribunal route, or human review route.

That makes this chapter the verification counterpart to context transactions.
The previous chapter asks whether the memory view is valid. This one asks
whether the system has enough checking capacity to use that view for the
claim now being made.

## Problem

The ASI Stack needs to distinguish access from understanding.

VCM can produce an authorized, coherent, versioned context packet. Context
transactions can preserve snapshots, mounts, taint, and deletion boundaries.
Those are necessary gates, but they do not answer a different question: can
this system verify this claim with this packet in this mode?

A context packet can be perfectly legitimate and still be inadequate. It may
contain the right documents but not enough active comparison budget. It may
contain a useful summary that dropped the exact constraint that matters. It
may support an orientation paragraph but not a safety claim. It may support a
local citation check but not a cross-document contradiction judgment.

Adequacy is therefore scoped. It is not a general property of a context
window. It is a relation among a context packet, a target claim, a risk tier,
and a verification mode. The same packet can be adequate for drafting and
inadequate for promotion.

## Why More Context Is Not Enough

Long context improves exposure. It does not automatically improve checking.

The `verification_bandwidth` source frames this as a bounded verification
problem. To check whether two semantic units agree, the system needs enough
effective workspace to compare them in detail. A large source can dominate
attention. A small but decisive contradiction can be present and still not be
checked. A chain of summaries can look locally coherent while hiding a global
conflict.

External long-context and evaluation work makes the same warning easier to
place. LongBench and RULER stress long-context behavior. Lost in the Middle
shows that placement inside a context window matters. LongLLMLingua highlights
compression tradeoffs. ALCE and Self-RAG separate generated answers from
retrieval, critique, and citation support. CheckList shows why aggregate
accuracy is too blunt for behavioral claims. GPQA exposes hard oversight
pressure, and Reluplex reminds us that property-specific verification is much
narrower than a broad safety story. These are comparators and vocabulary, not
evidence that this repository has run those evaluations.

The failure mode is long-context theater: because a system has many relevant
tokens in reach, it behaves as if the comparison has been performed. A reader
sees breadth and mistakes it for verification. The adequacy layer exists to
keep that mistake from becoming a support state.

The cure is not smaller context. It is scoped honesty. The system should say
when context is enough to orient, enough to draft, enough to check one local
relation, and not enough to carry a stronger claim.

## Core Claim

Context systems must distinguish generation capacity from verification
bandwidth and declare when context is inadequate. In the live book, this
chapter's core claim remains a design rationale at `argument` support.

Verification Bandwidth supplies the generation-versus-verification distinction,
semantic-unit comparison frame, dominant-component risk, transitive-decay
risk, and proposed contradiction-rate test. VCM supplies adequacy and
admission separation, use-relative context certificates, non-success states,
and the rule that summaries cannot recover omitted information. Spinoza
supplies the claim-ledger consequence: unsupported, unknown, or contradicted
claims should not be promoted. TreeLLM supplies a possible explicit
semantic-unit representation route. Editable VCM remains connector/source-note
refinement context, not a reproduced benchmark.

The current repo supports this claim only as architecture and record
discipline. It has a `context_adequacy_record` schema, public fixtures, a
synthetic context admission/adequacy harness, and finite Lean predicates for
admission-versus-adequacy separation and high-risk support blocking. It has
not measured model verification bandwidth, run the proposed contradiction-rate
experiment, run a distractor-resistance benchmark, proved attention behavior,
validated an adequacy classifier, or demonstrated deployed escalation.

## Mechanism

Verification bandwidth turns every important context packet into an adequacy
question.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Sources["Source units"] --> Packet["Admitted VCM packet"]
  Packet --> Units["Semantic-unit inventory"]
  Units --> Loss["Compression and loss record"]
  Loss --> Mode["Verification mode"]
  Mode --> Decision{"Adequate for target claim?"}
  Decision -- "yes" --> Support["Bounded claim support"]
  Decision -- "draft only" --> Draft["Drafting-only use"]
  Decision -- "no" --> Escalate["Retrieve, split, prove, review, or residualize"]
  Draft --> Ledger["Claim ledger"]
  Support --> Ledger
  Escalate --> Ledger
  Ledger --> Boundary["Support-state boundary"]
```

The adequacy record begins by naming the claim. Then it names the context
packet, the semantic units that matter, the compression path, the verification
mode, the risk tier, the negative evidence, and the residuals.

The key question is not "did the system read a lot?" The key question is
"what comparison did the system actually perform?" Direct lookup, pairwise
semantic comparison, schema validation, finite proof checking, tribunal
review, human review, and summary-only review are different modes. They should
not grant the same support.

Adequacy states make that difference visible:

- `absent`: required source units are not loaded or identified.
- `drafting_only`: the packet is useful for orientation or prose, not support.
- `local_check`: a narrow fact, citation, schema, or local relation was checked.
- `joint_check`: the required semantic units were compared in the declared mode.
- `summary_derived`: the claim rests on compressed material with known loss.
- `escalated`: the packet is inadequate and has been routed to stronger review.
- `contradicted`: the adequacy pass found a conflict that matters to the claim.

A claim can still be useful when its context is inadequate. It can be drafted,
queued, split, residualized, or escalated. What it cannot do is silently become
verified because the context window was large.

## A Contradiction That Was Present But Not Checked

Picture a long context packet with two documents. The first is long, recent,
and strongly worded. The second is short, older, and easy to miss, but it
contains a constraint that contradicts the first document's proposed
conclusion. A model may quote both documents in the same answer and still fail
to compare the two semantic units that matter.

The adequacy record should make that failure visible. It would name the target
claim, the relevant units in both documents, the compression path that carried
them into the packet, and the verification mode that was actually used. If the
system only summarized the long document and skimmed the short one, the record
should not say `joint_check`. It should say `drafting_only`,
`summary_derived`, or `escalated`, depending on what happened next.

The important point is not that the contradiction is hard to state. It is that
the contradiction can be present in context and still absent from verification.
Presence is exposure. Checking is work.

For low-risk prose, the honest output may be a cautious draft with a residual.
For a high-risk claim, the same packet should trigger retrieval, a pairwise
comparison, a proof or schema check when applicable, a split into narrower
claims, or human review. The adequacy layer keeps that choice attached to the
claim instead of letting a fluent synthesis hide the missing comparison.

## Interfaces

The central interface is the Context Adequacy Record. A useful record names:

- `adequacy_id`
- `target_claim_id`
- `claim_scope`
- `context_packet_ref`
- `context_scope`
- `risk_tier`
- `semantic_units`
- `compression_path`
- `verification_mode`
- `adequacy_state`
- `residual_risks`
- `negative_evidence`
- `required_escalation`
- `verification_artifact_refs`
- `audit_refs`
- `support_state_effect`
- `non_claims`

VCM declares packet admission and adequacy. Spinoza-style claim ledgers consume
the result before support-state movement. Planning decides whether to retrieve
more context, split the review, route to proof work, call a tribunal, ask a
human, or preserve a residual.

This interface prevents mode confusion. A schema check can prove that a record
has the right fields while leaving the underlying empirical claim unverified.
A Lean theorem can prove a finite predicate while leaving open whether the
predicate models a real system. A summary can preserve orientation while
dropping the constraint that decides a contradiction. The adequacy record keeps
those boundaries attached to the claim.

## Invariants

Adequacy must be explicit. If the system cannot say what level of checking the
context supports, the claim should not receive stronger support.

More context must not be treated as proof. A larger window may increase
available material while also increasing distractors, compression pressure,
and missed contradictions.

High-risk claims must pay a verification tax. If a claim can affect
governance, planning, deployment, safety, or support-state movement, drafting
adequacy is not enough.

Adequacy must be scoped to a target claim and verification mode. A packet
adequate for a chapter summary may be inadequate for a theorem claim, runtime
claim, benchmark claim, or governance claim.

Summary-derived support cannot exceed the declared loss and residual record.
Compression may be necessary, but it must not pretend to preserve every
constraint unless the preservation was actually checked.

## Failure Modes

Attention interference occurs when a large or salient unit suppresses checking
against a smaller unit that carries the decisive constraint.

False confidence from large context occurs when a system sounds more certain
because it had more text nearby, not because it performed stronger checks.

Verification skipped for cost reasons becomes dangerous when the skipped work
is later treated as if it had passed.

Adequacy laundering occurs when a packet adequate for drafting is reused as if
it were adequate for support promotion.

Mode confusion occurs when a local schema, proof, summary, retrieval, or human
review result is used to support a broader claim than it actually checked.

These failures are not solved by scolding the model to be careful. They need
records that make the skipped comparison visible.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is a context adequacy schema plus a fixture
suite.

The fixture suite should include admitted context that is adequate for
drafting but inadequate for verification, a high-risk claim blocked from
promotion, a summary-derived claim whose evidence ceiling is preserved, a
conflict that forces escalation, and a mode-confusion case where a local check
is prevented from supporting a broader claim.

The current repository has part of this minimum. Protocol validation checks
the public record shape. `scripts/validate_context_admission_adequacy.py`
checks synthetic cross-record fixtures for admission/adequacy separation,
escalation requirements, conflict blocking, stale certificate reuse, mode
confusion, and open deletion materialization. `AsiStackProofs.VerificationBandwidth`
contains finite predicates showing that an admitted packet may still be
inadequate and that a high-risk claim with inadequate context cannot receive a
verified support label.

The verification bandwidth contradiction probe adds a second narrow fixture. It
checks two synthetic adequacy traces: one where pairwise comparison finds a
planted cross-document contradiction and blocks verified support, and one where
the context is useful for drafting but not verification. It also rejects seven
bad paths, including summary-derived promotion, missed contradictions under a
dominant distractor, high-risk inadequate context without escalation, schema
checks treated as empirical runtime evidence, ignored negative evidence,
unidentified semantic units, and support-state promotion from the fixture. This
is not a model benchmark result.

The verification bandwidth capacity model adds a third narrow fixture. It
records a 12-unit all-pairwise case with 66 comparison obligations, 18 checked
obligations, and 48 residual obligations, plus a named decomposition case where
three clusters and six boundary checks reduce the modeled workload to 24
checks. Its negative controls reject treating linear context as pairwise
capacity, hiding boundary checks, hiding residuals, declaring adequacy without
identified units, and promoting a support state from the fixture. This is also
not a model benchmark.

That is useful evidence for record discipline. It is not evidence of model
verification capacity, contradiction-rate performance, distractor resistance,
summary fidelity, deployed VCM behavior, adequacy-classifier correctness, or
real escalation quality.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is a verification-capacity control plane.

In that endpoint, every important claim receives an adequacy record before it
can receive stronger support. The record says which semantic units were
available, which were compared, which were summarized, what was lost, what
negative evidence appeared, what verification mode ran, and what escalation
is required. The claim ledger then uses that record to block promotion,
preserve residuals, or route to stronger review.

Such a system would not reward long context by default. It would ask whether
the context was checkable. It would treat context length as exposure,
compression as risk, summaries as lossy artifacts unless proven otherwise, and
verification as a scarce budget that must be routed.

To reach that endpoint, the stack still needs adequacy classifiers,
contradiction-rate suites, distractor-resistance tests, summary-loss tests,
claim-ledger promotion gates, and review traces that show inadequate context
being downgraded or escalated in practice.

## Evidence Boundary

This chapter is a control-boundary argument, not a measured model law.

The live chapter remains at `argument` support. The current schemas, Lean
predicates, and synthetic harness support only declared-record behavior: they
show how the architecture should keep admission, adequacy, support labels,
escalation, and non-claims separate. They do not show that a model can
reliably recognize inadequate context, detect contradictions, resist
distractors, or verify long-context claims in the wild.

That boundary is essential. Verification bandwidth exists because systems are
tempted to treat access as understanding. The chapter should not commit the
same error about its own evidence.

## Summary

Verification bandwidth is the scarce capacity to compare the semantic units
that matter for a target claim. Context adequacy records whether the available
packet, compression path, verification mode, risk tier, and negative evidence
are enough for that claim.

The chapter's practical rule is that exposure is not support. Long context can
help a system draft, search, and orient, but stronger claims require explicit
checking. When the check is missing, the architecture should downgrade, split,
escalate, or residualize rather than sounding certain.

## Handoff

Adequacy records need a durable destination. Otherwise, they remain comments
around a claim rather than part of the claim's life.

Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision receives the adequacy record and turns it
into claim identity, support state, contradiction links, revision history,
uncertainty, residuals, and promotion conditions. That is how the stack turns
"we could not verify this" into evidence that future work can act on.
